behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Grouping by trace ID is a pure data transformation algorithm with no I/O, external dependencies, or OS-level behavior. A unit test passing real (in-memory) log entry objects through the grouping function will catch algorithmic bugs like incorrect field comparison, hash collisions, or grouping logic errors. No mocks needed; the data structures themselves are the test payload.
behaviors.test_description: Create test log entries from multiple services with identical and distinct trace IDs. Pass through grouping function. Verify: (1) all entries with same trace ID are in one group, (2) entries with different trace IDs are in separate groups, (3) service origin does not affect grouping.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test sufficient because grouping failure is purely algorithmic—bugs in the comparison or hash logic will surface immediately with real data structures.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Clock skew handling is a sorting and comparison algorithm operating on in-memory timestamp data. No I/O, external calls, or OS-specific behavior. A unit test with real (in-memory) log objects with various timestamp offsets will expose bugs in the sorting logic, threshold calculations, or out-of-order handling.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with timestamps: (1) correct order, (2) out of order, (3) within 500ms skew bounds, (4) exceeding 500ms skew, (5) edge cases at exactly 500ms. Pass to timeline function. Verify entries are ordered correctly and skew is properly handled.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test catches ordering algorithm failures. No real time sources or I/O needed to test the skew-handling logic.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: P99 percentile calculation and threshold comparison are pure statistical algorithms on in-memory numeric data. No I/O, external calls, or non-determinism. Unit tests with known latency datasets (constructed to have specific p99 values) will catch bugs in percentile computation, baseline calculation, or threshold logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create latency datasets with: (1) baseline state (low variance), (2) 2.5x spike (below threshold), (3) 3x+ spike (above threshold), (4) edge case exactly at 3x threshold, (5) datasets with different distributions. Verify anomaly detection correctly flags only 3x+ spikes.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test sufficient for statistical algorithm. Real failure (incorrect p99 or threshold logic) is purely computational.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: While JSON parsing error handling logic is unit-testable, the real failure mode involves reading actual log files or streams that contain malformed entries. The pipeline must handle I/O errors gracefully while processing a stream of mixed valid/invalid data. Testing with only string literals misses failures in stream handling, buffering, or file-reading error recovery. Integration-level testing with real I/O is needed to verify the full ingestion pipeline doesn't crash and correctly counts skipped entries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test log file or data stream containing mix of valid JSON and malformed entries (invalid syntax, truncated data, encoding issues). Run full ingestion pipeline. Verify: (1) pipeline completes without crashing, (2) malformed entries are skipped, (3) skip count matches number of malformed entries, (4) all valid entries are processed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration level necessary because mocking file I/O hides real stream-handling failures. The failure boundary is the I/O layer, not just the parser logic.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Throughput under load depends on real I/O performance, OS buffering, file descriptor limits, thread scheduling, and memory management—not in-process logic. An in-memory dataset test wouldn't expose I/O bottlenecks, file descriptor exhaustion, or network backpressure. Real I/O (file or network source) is required to measure actual throughput, detect dropped entries due to buffer overflow, and verify the tool keeps pace under sustained load.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or stream log entries from file/network at 10,000/sec for sustained period (e.g., 10+ seconds). Measure: (1) total entries processed, (2) whether any entries are dropped or lost, (3) pipeline latency (time from ingestion to output). Verify count matches input count and latency remains acceptable.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level required to test real I/O performance. Integration-level in-memory tests would show the algorithm can process 10k items, but not whether the I/O layer can sustain 10k/sec throughput.
